coredata.dc:title: A colorimetric platform for sensitive sensing of Hg2+ and S2− based on Se-AuNPs with Hg2+-activated peroxidase-like activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaorong
Yang, Dezhi
Li, Qiulan
Zhong, Zitao
Li, Hong
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the selenium (Se) modified gold nanoparticles (Se-AuNPs) was synthesized using cerium doped carbon dots (Ce-CDs) as a reducing agent and template. As desired, Se-AuNPs displays enhanced peroxidase (POD)-like activity in the presence of Hg2+. The mechanism for the enhanced activity was attributed to the increased affinity between Se-AuNPs-Hg2+ and the substrate, in which Se and Au elements have a strong binding capacity to Hg2+, forming Hg–Se bonds and Au–Hg amalgam to generate more ·OH. This POD-like activity of Se-AuNPs-Hg2+ correlates with the colorimetric reaction by the catalytic reaction between 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and H2O2. The oxidation of TMB was completely inhibited by the introduction of the reductive S2−. Based on the above findings, a strategy for the colorimetric detection of Hg2+ and S2− by Se-AuNPs was established with linear ranges of 0.33–66 μg/L and 0.625–75 μg/L, and low detection limits of 0.17 μg/L and 0.12 μg/L (3.3 δ/k), respectively. When the colorimetric probes for detection of Hg2+ and S2− was applied in environmental water samples, the recoveries were in the range of 90.3–108.0 %. This method will provide a new idea for the colorimetric detection strategy of Hg2+ due to the strong interaction between Hg and Se.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Se-AuNPs
Hg2+-activated peroxidase-like activity
Hg2+
S2−
Colorimetric detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: POD-like activity of Se-AuNPs and Ce-CDs/AuNPs
Colorimetric detection of Hg2+ and S2−
Recognition mechanisms of Hg2+ and S2-
Detection of Hg2+ and S2− in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Se-AuNPs; (b) HAADF-STEM, elemental mapping images of Ce, Au and Se; (c) FTIR spectra of nanozymes; (d) XRD spectra of nanozymes; (e–h) XPS spectra of nanozymes.
(a) Absorption spectra of different substrates being oxidized; (b) Absorption spectra of TMB being oxidized; (c) UV–vis-kinetics of Ce-CDs/AuNPs; (d) UV–vis-kinetics of Se-AuNPs.

(a) Absorption spectra with the different concentration of Hg2+; (b) linear curve of Hg2+ concentration; (c) Absorption spectra with the different concentration of S2−; (d) linear curve of S2− concentration.
(a) Sensitive of Se-AuNPs system for Hg2+ detection; (b) Sensitive of Se-AuNPs system for S2− detection.

(a) Zeta potential of Se-AuNPs with the addition of Hg2+ and S2−; (b) Fluorescence detection of ∙OH; (c) Verification of UV–vis spectra of ∙OH production in the reaction system with IPA; (d) EPR spectra.
Schematic illustration of the sensing of Hg2+ and S2− involving the Se-AuNPs.
Comparison of the Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximum reaction rate (V max) for TMB as substrate.

head.ce:title: A colorimetric platform for sensitive sensing of Hg2+ and S2− based on Se-AuNPs with Hg2+-activated peroxidase-like activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Yunnan Agricultural University, Kunming, 650201, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The author contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the selenium (Se) modified gold nanoparticles (Se-AuNPs) was synthesized using cerium doped carbon dots (Ce-CDs) as a reducing agent and template. As desired, Se-AuNPs displays enhanced peroxidase (POD)-like activity in the presence of Hg2+. The mechanism for the enhanced activity was attributed to the increased affinity between Se-AuNPs-Hg2+ and the substrate, in which Se and Au elements have a strong binding capacity to Hg2+, forming Hg–Se bonds and Au–Hg amalgam to generate more ·OH. This POD-like activity of Se-AuNPs-Hg2+ correlates with the colorimetric reaction by the catalytic reaction between 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and H2O2. The oxidation of TMB was completely inhibited by the introduction of the reductive S2−. Based on the above findings, a strategy for the colorimetric detection of Hg2+ and S2− by Se-AuNPs was established with linear ranges of 0.33–66 μg/L and 0.625–75 μg/L, and low detection limits of 0.17 μg/L and 0.12 μg/L (3.3 δ/k), respectively. When the colorimetric probes for detection of Hg2+ and S2− was applied in environmental water samples, the recoveries were in the range of 90.3–108.0 %. This method will provide a new idea for the colorimetric detection strategy of Hg2+ due to the strong interaction between Hg and Se.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes are nanomaterials that mimic enzyme-like catalytic activity, and have attracted a lot of attention as substitutes for natural enzymes in various research fields, due to their intrinsic physicochemical properties, including low cost, ease of preparation, and good stability [1,2]. Nanozymes including metal-, noble metal-, metal oxide- and carbon-based nanomaterials have been used in biochemical sensing [3], pollution degradation [4], environmental monitoring [5] and immunoassays as well as biomedical applications [6,7]. Particularly for sensing, some nanozyme-based colorimetric analyses using catalytic colorimetric signal amplification techniques can reach low detection limits at the nM level [8–10]. Typical nanoscale factors such as size, morphology, and the surface property of the nanozymes significantly influence their activity, which provide excellent strategies to modulate their activity. Of these, size and surface coating may be more dominant, as catalytic reactions usually occur on the surface of the nanozyme [3]. Due to the high surface-to-volume ratio, small nanomaterials can be easily expected to high catalytic activity nanozymes. AuNPs are the most representative nanomaterials among noble metal nanomaterials, and it is possible that the same noble metal nanoparticles possess one or more types of enzyme activities [11–13]. However, the enzyme activity and stability of AuNPs are relatively weak and their application is limited, so better surface ligands need to be added to improve their enzyme activity [14–16]. Carbon dots (CDs) have excellent chemical stability, low toxicity, and easy surface modification. Meanwhile, they have ultra-thin layer structures, tiny lateral dimensions, and abundant surface functional groups, which can be coated with noble metal nanoparticles (e.g. Au and Ag) [17,18]. Previous research groups have demonstrated that CDs/metal nanocomposites possess nanozyme activity [19].

In recent years, the increasing severity of the water pollution problem has made people realize the importance of water resource management and better use of water resources [20,21]. Mercury ions (Hg2+) and sulfur ions (S2−), which are toxic and harmful, are essential for the monitoring of many diseases [22]. Hg2+ has been recognized as one of the most toxic chemicals to human health, causing irreversible damage to the central nervous system and kidneys [23–26]. S2− can be easily converted to H2S in acidic media, and hydrogen sulfide is a highly toxic substance that can be harmful to the eyes and respiratory system, etc. Therefore, the development of efficient S2− and Hg2+ detection methods is an imminent task [20]. Accordingly, the World Health Organization (WHO) recommends a limit of 3 mg/L of Hg2+ [27] and 0.6 mmol/L of S2− [28] in drinking water.

In previous work, we synthesized CDs-based AuNPs for the detection of Hg2+ and methylmercury by adsorption of Hg onto the surface of AuNPs to form alloy aggregates, which achieved the enhancement of catalytic enzyme activity of AuNPs [29]. However, Au–Hg is unstable due to instability of AuNPs, Se–Hg is more stable and the surface properties, morphology and stability of AuNPs can be modulated with selenium-based ligands. Although sulfur (S) and Se share similar properties, Se ligands have the greater advantages. Se breaks the Au–S bond to produce the more stable Au–Se bond, suggesting that Au–Se bonds form more readily and are more stable in biological thiol microenvironments. Se is non-toxic and the strong affinity between Se and Hg is commonly used for Hg2+ contamination removal, which can be converted into stable, low solubility, low toxicity precipitates. Based on the co-incubation of Na2SeO3 and cerium-doped carbon dots (Ce-CDs) with HAuCl4 to form more stable Au–Se bonds on the surface of AuNPs nanohybrids (Se-AuNPs) by using CDs as the reducing agents and templates. In this paper, Se-AuNPs nanozyme with weak peroxidase activity were prepared using carbon dots as reducing agent and sodium citrate as stabilizer, and then an efficient detection of Hg2+ and S2− colorimetric sensing method was built based on the Hg-promoted POD-like activity of Se-AuNPs (Scheme 1 ): weak POD-like activity of Se-AuNPs using TMB as substrate; the addition of Hg2+ enhances the oxidation of TMB due to the formation of Au–Hg amalgams and Hg–Se bonds with it, which stimulates and significantly increases the POD-like activity; additionally, the sensing system further enables the detection of S2−, which is reductive and reduces the blue oxTMB to colorless. The enhanced POD-like activity of Se-AuNPs, and its distinct recognition mechanisms for Hg2+ and S2− were also systemically studied.

Firstly, the Ce-CDs was synthesized by microwave ablation by stirring Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, DA(Dopamine), CA(citric acid), EDA(Ethylenediamine) and deionized water thoroughly, transferring them to a PTFE(polytetrafluoroethylene) tank. Then irradiating the reactor with 850 W microwave to raise the temperature to 180 °C within 1 min for 1 h. Finally, after cooling, it was filtered through a membrane and set aside.

Briefly, 30 mL of DI water, 200 μL of HAuCl4 (1 %) and 30 μL of the above synthesized Ce-CDs (0.2 mg/mL) were mixed at 95 °C for 5 min. Subsequently, 300 μL of trisodium citrate (0.1 M) and 600 μL of 1 % Na2SeO3 were mixed simultaneously, Finally, stirring was continued at 95 °C for 20 min, resulting in a purplish-red color solution of Se-AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: POD-like activity of Se-AuNPs and Ce-CDs/AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of Se-AuNPs was evaluated through colorimetric reaction of oxidized classical substrates TMB, OPD (o-Phenylenediamine) and ABTS (2, 2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)). 100 μL of substrate (5 mM), 100 μL of H2O2 (50 mM), and 100 μL of Se-AuNPs were added to the test tubes.

In order to reveal the kinetic study of the enzymatic reaction of Se-AuNPs as a peroxide mimetic enzyme, the changes in absorbance values were measured at 654 nm in a POD-like reaction using TMB as substrate. The Michaelis-Menten constant K m and the maximum reaction rate V max were calculated by double inverse method.
1 v
=
K m
V max
×
1
[ S ]
+
1
V max
where v is the initial velocity, [S] is the concentration of substrate, K m is the Michaelis-Menten constant, and V max is the maximum velocity.
The same method described above was used to study the kinetic of Ce-CDs/AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of Hg2+ and S2−
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of Hg2+: The reaction of Se-AuNPs oxidizing TMB under H2O2 conditions was added with different concentrations (0.33–66 μg/L) of Hg2+ solution and the reaction was incubated at 25 °C for 15 min, followed by determination at the UV wavelength of 654 nm.

Detection of S2−: To the system containing the above Hg2+, S2− was added at different concentrations (0.625–75 μg/L) and the reaction was incubated at 25 °C for 15 min, followed by determination at the UV wavelength of 654 nm.

To test the real samples and calculate the recovery using the colorimetric method developed in this experiment, three water samples, namely, industrial wastewater, lake water and seawater, were selected. Each of the three water samples will be measured in 10 mL, separated by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 30 min, then removed by membrane filtration.

The TEM images show that the Se-AuNPs have irregular morphology, large aggregated fusion of particles, and nanostructures with a narrow size distribution with an average size of about 10–12 nm. The HRTEM images of the Se-AuNPs show that the lattice fringe spacing is 0.24 nm, which matches well with the crystallographic spacing of Au (111) (Fig. 1 a). The formation of Au–Se bonds can also be observed from the TEM image (Fig. 1a), while the elemental mapping (Fig. 1b) shows the presence of Au and Se in the Se-AuNPs sample. This result can prove the successful synthesis of the material.

The FTIR spectra of Se-AuNPs and Se-AuNPs + Hg (Fig. 1c) showed that the IR peaks appeared at 3423 cm−1 for OH bonds, 1586 cm−1 for C=C bonds, 1396 cm−1 for C–H and OH bonds, 1078 cm−1 for C–O bonds, and 840 cm−1 for the stretching vibrations of C–H bonds, which were attributed to the presence of DA and TA compounds. In the XRD spectra of Se-AuNPs and Se-AuNPs + Hg (Fig. 1d), the 2θ peaks appeared at 38.1°, 44.3°, 64.5°, and 77.7°, which are typical peaks attributed to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) facets of AuNPs, respectively [30].

In order to further verify the existence of Se–Au bonds and the changes before and after the addition of Hg, the compositions of the surface components of the Se-AuNPs matrix were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The results are shown in Fig. 1e–h.

Se 3d peaks were observed in the XPS spectra of Se obtained from the Na2SeO3 compounds, 59.2 eV corresponds to Se 3d5/2, and 63.4 eV corresponds to the binding energy of Se 3d3/2 (Fig. 1f), which coincide with the previously reported binding values of Se with Au. This result also indicates that more stable Au–Se bonds were formed on the surface of Se-AuNPs in the present method. In addition, the Se 3d spectrum can be separated into two different peaks that can be attributed to Se2− and Se0, respectively (Fig. 1f), and this result is in general agreement with the analytical results of the XRD spectra. The Au 4f XPS spectrum shows two peaks (Fig. 1g): the binding energies of Se-AuNPs at 83.4 eV and 87.1 eV verify the presence of Au0 and the reduction of the Au precursor. The XPS peaks of Hg 4f can be observed at 100.2 and 104.3 eV (Fig. 1h). These results are consistent with the results of FTIR analysis, both of which can prove the successful preparation of Se-AuNPs.

The POD-like activities of Se-AuNPs were evaluated by typical chromogenic substrates. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the catalytic oxidation of colorless TMB, ABTS and OPD by Se-AuNPs in the presence of H2O2 produced the corresponding blue, green and yellow products, which corresponded to the characteristic UV absorption peaks of 654, 416 and 421 nm, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 2b, in the presence of H2O2, Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Se-AuNPs catalyzed the oxidation of colorless TMB to form blue oxTMB, with an obvious absorption characteristic peak at 654 nm. From the Figure, it can be found that the peak of Se-AuNPs catalytic oxidation of TMB is significantly higher than the absorbance of Ce-CDs/AuNPs catalytic oxidation. The above results also show that this colorimetric study method has low noise signal and excellent performance.

In order to reveal the kinetic study of the enzymatic reaction of Se-AuNPs as peroxidase mimics, the catalytic reaction of POD-like enzyme activity was monitored by using a UV–visible kinetic model based on the relevant literature. The catalytic mechanism of Se-AuNPs-like POD-like activity was determined by varying the concentration of the substrates, TMB and H2O2, and immobilizing Se-AuNPs to perform a steady-state kinetic assay. It is well known that small K m values calculated from UV–visible kinetic assays indicate high substrate affinity. The K m of 0.25 mM and the V max of 2.21 × 10−8 M s−1 were calculated from UV–visible absorption measurements(Fig. 2c and d). Compared with other nanozymes and HRP [30], Se-AuNPs has lower K m and higher V max, indicating Se-AuNPs has a stronger affinity with substrates (Table 1 ).

TMB was studied as a substrate, and Figs. S1a–c show the optimal reaction conditions (temperature of 40 °C, pH = 4 and reaction time of 20 min). The switching strategy is based on the fact that Se–Hg bonds and gold amalgams can be formed between Hg2+ and Se-AuNPs, which accelerates the electron transfer [33]. Using the designed study method, the absorbance values at 654 nm increased with increasing Hg2+ concentration and the color of the solution changed from colorless to blue (Fig. 3 a and b); based on the data obtained, a linear plot of absorbance values versus Hg2+ concentration was derived for 0.33–66 μg/L(Fig. 3b), and the fitted linear equation was Abs = 0.006 [Hg2+](μg/L) + 0.359(R2 = 0.990). According to the formula 3.3σ/k (where 3.3 is the 99 % confidence coefficient, δ is the standard deviation of 20 blank experiments, and k is the slope of the linear regression equation), the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.17 μg/L. It is lower than the maximum allowable level of Hg2+ in ambient water, which was 5 μg/L according to the regulation of GB/T 14848-93 [24]. As shown in Table S1, the method proposed in this study showed higher sensitivity and a comparably low to those of the reported other assays. The absorbance decreased with increasing S2− concentration when S2− was added again to form HgS(Fig. 3c and d); when the S2−concentration was in the range of 0.625–75 μg/L, the absorbance value decreased gradually and was linearly related to the S2− concentration(Fig. 3d), and the fitted linear equation was Abs = 0.0129 [S2−](μg/L) + 1.4149(R2 = 0.992). The detection limit of 0.12 μg/L was obtained from 3.3σ/k, which is lower than the maximum allowable level of S2− in water specified in GB/T 1.1–2000 [28]. As shown in Table S2, compared with other reported assays, the method has good specificity and has lower detection limit. The developed colorimetric assay based on Se-AuNPs is highly accurate and sensitive in the analysis of Hg2+ and S2−.

For this study, some possible coexisting substances were added into the system to verify the selectivity of this method. As shown in Fig. 4 a, all the possible coexisting ions concentrations are 50 times the concentration of Hg2+(10 μg/L), and the results in the graph show that the addition of Hg2+ shows stronger absorbance values at 654 nm. As shown in Fig. 4b, it is clear that the present method has a high specificity for the detection of S2−. Therefore, the colorimetric sensing system of Se-AuNPs can specifically identify Hg2+ and S2− in real water sample environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition mechanisms of Hg2+ and S2-
ce:section.ce:para: This can be illustrated by the ζ potential: Hg2+ promotes the enzymatic activity of Se-AuNPs and S2− reduces the absorbance of Se-AuNPs + Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the Zeta potentials of Se-AuNPs, Se-AuNPs + Hg2+and Se-AuNPs + Hg2++S2− are −30.5 mV, −27.9 mV and −47.3 mV, respectively. The above results indicated that the negative charge carried on the surface of Se-AuNPs combines easily with the positively charged Hg2+ to form Se–Hg bonds and gold amalgam.

The main reason for nanomaterials with POD-like activity is due to the generation of free radicals (ROS). Terephthalic acid (TA) can be catalytically oxidized by nanozymes to produce fluorescence to detect the presence of hydroxyl radicals (∙OH) in the system, and the more ∙OH produced during the reaction, the higher the fluorescence intensity. The results (Fig. 5b) demonstrated that the fluorescence emission at 432 nm after 1 h of co-incubation of TA and H2O2 alone was very weak and negligible. Whereas for the TA + H2O2+Se-AuNPs system, the fluorescence intensity was significantly increased, which indicated that the Se-AuNPs could convert H2O2 into ∙OH in the catalytic process.

The mechanism of Se-AuNPs-like POD activity was investigated using free radical scavenging and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments, and IPA was used as a scavenger to remove ∙OH. As shown in Fig. 5c, the peak decreases after the addition of IPA to the Se-AuNPs system compared to the control (without scavenger), which indicates the production of ∙OH in the catalytic reaction of the Se-AuNPs system. As displayed in Fig. 5d, the EPR spectra indicate that Se-AuNPs, Se-AuNPs-Hg2+ and Se-AuNPs-Hg2++S2− nanozymes-mediated systems have finally generated ·OH. Significantly, the intensities of ·OH in the Se-AuNPs-Hg2+ system are highest than that in the two nanozymes systems, which demonstrated that Hg2+ activated the peroxidase-like activity of Se-AuNPs. In conclusion, due to the strong affinity binding between Se and Hg2+, it can bind with AuNPs to form Au–Hg amalgams and Hg–Se bonds, which may change the surface properties of Se-AuNPs and thus promote the production of more ∙OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and S2− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The samples were pretreated, and then different standard concentrations (0.5, 5 and 15 μg L-1) of Hg2+ were added to the actual water samples. The recoveries of Hg2+ were in the range of 89.15 %–108.00 % (as shown in Table S3). The RSD value of the colorimetric method was less than 3.90 %, indicating a good precision. This method is suitable for the analysis of Hg2+ in aqueous environment.

After the samples were processed, the water samples were analyzed and verified by adding different standard concentrations of S2− to them. The results are shown in Table S4. The recoveries of S2− in the samples ranged from 94.70 % to 108.00 %, indicating that the assay is capable of S2− detection in the aqueous environment. The RSD values of UV detection were less than 5.02 %, indicating that the method has good precision.

In summary, we have successfully developed a Hg2+ and S2− colorimetric detection method using Se-AuNPs as a probe. Se-AuNPs was first successfully prepared using cerium doped carbon dots (Ce-CDs) as a reducing agent and template and Se modified. Se has a strong affinity for Hg due to the biological antagonism between Se and Hg2+. Then the POD-like activity of Se-AuNPs was found to be sensitively and selectively activated by Hg2+, which was attributed to the formation of Hg–Se bonds and Au–Hg amalgam. Besides, the Hg2+-activated POD-like activity of Se-AuNPs exhibited excellent catalytic ability and good stability for the oxidation process of TMB. Based on this phenomenon, a novel nanozyme colorimetric probe developed for the detection of Hg2+ and S2− possessed high sensitivities with LODs of 0.17 μg/L for Hg2+ and 0.12 μg/L for S2− with good reproducibility and accuracy. The developed colorimetric approach offers a new and versatile strategy for the determination of Hg2+ and S2− in environmental water samples.

Xiaorong Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Dezhi Yang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Qiulan Li: Methodology. Zitao Zhong: Software. Hong Li: Software. Yaling Yang: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no competing interests to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC Grant No. 32302210), Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202201AU070117).
